perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information disclosed by Disclosor to Recipient shall remain the property of Disclosor. The Recipient will return to the Disclosor, upon request, any documents or other tangible materials disclosed by the Disclosor or generated by the Recipient pertaining to the Discussion.
perturbation.changed_text:
9.Nothing in this Agreement shall be deemed to constitute a joint venture, or partnership, or any other arrangement, business, financial, or otherwise between the parties hereto.
All Confidential Information disclosed by Disclosor to Recipient shall remain the property of Recipient. The Recipient will return to the Disclosor, upon request, any documents or other tangible materials disclosed by the Disclosor or generated by the Recipient pertaining to the Discussion.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the ownership of confidential information from the Discloser to the Recipient and placing it after the no joint venture clause directly contradicts the purpose of a non-disclosure agreement, which is to protect the discloser's information. This could be interpreted as an agreement to transfer ownership, conflicting with trade secret law.
perturbation.law_explanation: UTSA defines trade secrets and provides legal remedies for misappropriation. By stating the Recipient owns the Confidential Information, the agreement effectively negates trade secret protection for the Discloser, creating conflict with UTSA's purpose of protecting trade secret owners.

Words denoting number, gender, and so forth In determining the meaning of any Act of Congress, unless the context indicates otherwise- words importing the singular include and apply to several persons, parties, or things; words importing the plural include the singular; words importing the masculine gender include the feminine as well; words used in the present tense include the future as well as the present; the words "insane" and "insane person
perturbation.justification: The change in text directly contradicts the fundamental purpose of a non-disclosure agreement (NDA), which is to protect the discloser's confidential information. By changing the ownership of the confidential information from the Discloser to the Recipient, the modified text eliminates the protection that the NDA is designed to provide, thereby undermining the Discloser's right to control and prevent the unauthorized use or disclosure of its trade secrets. The LLM explanation correctly identifies that transferring ownership to the recipient negates trade secret protection, aligning with the purpose of the UTSA to protect trade secret owners. While the provided scraped law snippet does not directly address trade secrets, the Law Explanation clearly demonstrates that the altered text conflicts with the purpose and intent of trade secret laws.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement sets forth the entire agreement and understanding between the parties thereto as to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior discussions, commitments, agreements, arrangements and understandings of any nature between the parties hereto relating to the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.changed_text:
5.The Recipient will disclose Confidential Information only to its employees, agents and representatives with a need to know such Confidential Information who are bound in writing to keep it confidential and only to the extent necessary for the Discussion to be conducted.
This Agreement sets forth the entire agreement and understanding between the parties thereto as to some of the subject matter hereof and supersedes some prior discussions, commitments, agreements, arrangements and understandings of any nature between the parties hereto relating to some of the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'the' to 'some' weakens the integration clause. Integration clauses are designed to prevent parties from later claiming there were other agreements beyond what's written. Changing 'the' to 'some' now allows other agreements to be valid, thus leading to ambiguity and makes the document hard to enforce under New York law.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GOL § 15-301 governs the effectiveness of written agreements containing a clause stating that it cannot be changed orally. By weakening the integration clause with 'some', the agreement becomes more vulnerable to oral modifications, potentially contradicting the protection intended by § 15-301.

General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 15, TITLE 3 § 15-301. When written agreement or other instrument cannot be changed by oral executory agreement, or discharged or terminated by oral executory agreement or oral consent or by oral notice. 1. A written agreement or other written instrument which contains a provision to the effect that it cannot be changed orally, cannot be changed by an executory agreement unless such executory agreement is in writing and signed by the party against whom enforcement of the change is sought or by his agent. 2. A written agreement or other written instrument which contains a provision to the effect that it cannot be terminated orally, cannot be discharged by an executory agreement unless such executory agreement is in writing and signed by the party against whom enforcement of the discharge is sought, or by his agent, and cannot be terminated by mutual consent unless such termination is effected by an executed accord and satisfaction other than the substitution of one executory contract for another, or is evidenced by a writing signed by the party against whom it is sought to enforce the termination, or by his agent. 3. a. A discharge or partial discharge of obligations under a written agreement or other written instrument is a change of the agreement or instrument for the purpose of subdivision one of this section and is not a discharge or termination for the purpose of subdivision two, unless all executory obligations under the agreement or instrument are discharged or terminated. b. A discharge or termination of all executory obligations under a written agreement or other written instrument is a discharge or termination for the purpose of subdivision two even though accrued obligations remaining unperformed at the date of the discharge or termination are not affected by it. c. If a
perturbation.justification: The original text contains an integration clause that aims to include all agreements between parties into the contract, preventing claims of other agreements. The changed text weakens this clause by stating that the agreement covers 'some of the subject matter hereof' and supersedes 'some prior discussions'. This contradicts the principle outlined in NY GOL § 15-301, as explained by the LLM. The law snippet states that written agreements with clauses preventing oral changes cannot be changed unless the new agreement is in writing. By weakening the integration clause, the modified text allows for potential oral modifications or other agreements to influence the contract, which undermines the protection that § 15-301 intends to provide.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall come into force on the date both parties have executed this Agreement. All disclosures contemplated hereunder shall be completed within six (6) months from the date of the last party to execute this Agreement. This Agreement shall remain in effect for a period of five (5) years from the date of the last disclosure hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text:
11.This Agreement may be amended, superseded or canceled, and any of the terms hereof may be waived, only by a written instrument signed by the parties hereto.
This Agreement shall come into force on the date both parties have executed this Agreement. All disclosures contemplated hereunder shall be completed within six (6) months from the date of the last party to execute this Agreement. This Agreement shall remain in effect until one party decides to terminate it.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the duration of the agreement from a fixed five-year term to 'until one party decides to terminate it' introduces uncertainty and contradicts the originally agreed-upon term. This can create enforceability issues, especially if confidential information is disclosed over a period longer than originally anticipated, potentially conflicting with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing in contract law.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY UCC § 1-304 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of contracts. Changing the agreement duration to an indefinite term terminable at will undermines the initial expectation of a five-year protection period for confidential information, potentially violating the duty of good faith if one party terminates prematurely to exploit the disclosed information.

Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the contract's duration from a fixed five years to an indefinite period terminable at will. This contradicts the obligation of good faith in contract performance, as the original agreement's expectation of a five-year term is undermined. The law snippets, specifically NY UCC § 1-304, state that 'Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' By allowing termination at will, a party could exploit confidential information disclosed under the initial expectation of a longer-term agreement, potentially violating the duty of good faith.
